Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time during the week, but with limited resources and having to balance extracurriculars, I ended up studying whenever I could grab a moment. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often be doing things physically—writing notes, practicing problems—rather than just reading. I averaged maybe 19 hours a week, but those hours were spread out unpredictably across different days.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due. I didn't have a set schedule like 'study every Tuesday at 3pm'—it was more reactive. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more hours in. Other weeks when things felt calmer, I might barely touch the material. My attendance was pretty low because I often felt I could catch up through the online materials instead.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any gamified tasks available because those actually made studying feel less tedious. The points and badges system gave me a little boost of motivation even when I wasn't feeling particularly driven to study.
Q4: Yeah, after a while logging into the LMS became kind of automatic, especially when I got into the gamified tasks. I'd check in almost daily just to see my progress and if there were new challenges. It wasn't like I forced myself—it just became a habit because there was something to look forward to, even if I didn't always complete the actual assignments.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful for me—I watched a lot of them, probably over 177 minutes total. As a kinesthetic learner, I'd watch and then try to replicate or practice what I saw. The discussion forums were also useful because interacting with peers helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. Those felt like active learning rather than passive reading.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often ignored or barely touched the reading materials and some of the longer written assignments. With my limited resources, I couldn't always print things out or access them comfortably offline. Plus, reading dense text without being able to do something with it just didn't stick for me. I'd skim them at best.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually do something with it—apply it, practice it, or get immediate feedback. The gamified tasks were great because I could see my progress right away. If something was just passive consumption with no interaction, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to feel like I was building toward something tangible.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which weirdly made me more likely to try them sometimes. Required ones carried this weight of 'you have to do this or else,' which stressed me out even though my stress levels were generally low. With optional stuff, especially the gamified ones, I could engage at my own pace without that pressure.
Q9: Oh definitely. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had good internet access and then study them offline later. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd work through problems by hand—that's how I really learn. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS, using notes I'd made or practicing with materials I'd saved.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd download a lecture video once and watch it multiple times offline, or when I'd spend hours working through practice problems in my notebook. I also studied with classmates in person sometimes, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then study from it for days without logging back in. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could. The LMS would show one access, but that material might have been the basis for hours of offline study.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement got harder, usually when multiple things piled up at once—extracurriculars, personal stuff, other courses. My resilience isn't great, so when things got overwhelming, I'd kind of shut down a bit. Surprisingly though, the gamified tasks helped pull me back in during those times because they felt manageable and gave me small wins.
Q13: Workload from other areas definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted or had competing responsibilities, I'd do the bare minimum—maybe just log in, check what was urgent, and log out. I wouldn't engage deeply with discussions or explore extra materials. My task completion was only around 47% partly because of this juggling act.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in, maybe scrolling through a video, but my mind was elsewhere. It looked like engagement on paper but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine studying.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even important ones. My assignment completion was pretty low because I'd tell myself I'd do it later, then later never came. Part of it was low motivation—I just couldn't muster the energy sometimes. The gamified tasks were easier to do because they felt less daunting than big assignments.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS. Without that pressure, I'd drift. My whole engagement pattern was pretty deadline-driven—lots of activity right before something was due, then quiet periods in between. It wasn't ideal but it's how I functioned.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me when I got it. Positive feedback or seeing my scores improve in the gamified system made me want to engage more. But when feedback was delayed or I got a disappointing grade, I'd kind of withdraw for a bit. My mood after the gamified intervention was really high, which shows how much that immediate feedback helped.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having online materials available was crucial since my attendance was low, but the structure also assumed consistent engagement that I couldn't always maintain. The gamified elements added structure that actually worked for me—clear goals, visible progress, immediate rewards. That helped more than traditional assignment structures.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they broke things into smaller, achievable pieces. What made it harder was when assignments were large and vague, or when there wasn't clear guidance on what to prioritize. Also, some resources assumed you had reliable internet or could attend sessions, which wasn't always my reality with limited resources.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions, but struggling to actually submit the formal work. The informal engagement feels more accessible and less high-stakes. Submitting assignments requires a different kind of energy and organization that doesn't always match up with browsing activity.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was pretty good even though my attendance and assignment completion were low. I think some students, myself included, can learn effectively through concentrated effort and alternative methods that don't show up in activity logs. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything that contributes to understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you engage. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline learning. They can't tell if someone watched a video actively or had it playing in the background. They definitely miss the notebook work, the peer discussions outside the platform, the thinking that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and putting effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, when I want to figure something out, when I'm actively processing information rather than just consuming it. For me, engagement is hands-on—doing something with the material, not just passively receiving it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the gamified tasks actually shifted my mood and motivation. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions or the physical notes I made. It also wouldn't show the surprise I felt when certain approaches actually worked for me, or how I'd sometimes be deeply engaged mentally while appearing inactive online. The human experience of learning is way more complex than login data.
